text: STRUCTURE OF PARANORMAL EXPERIENCES DAVID METCALFE In theory, science should welcome anomalies as the harbingers of new discoveries. The scientific intelligence should derive joy from being surprised by new and hitherto unexplained phenomena. After all, isn’t that what science is all about? —Joseph M. Felser, Outsiders, Anomalies, and the Future of “Forbidden Science” ALONG THE ROAD THERE CAME A STRANGER The twentieth century’s iconic Fortean adventurer, John Keel, had been writing about UFOs since 1945 as a journalist for various publications, but it was while inquiring into a series of anomalous sightings in Point Pleasant, West Virginia, in the 1970s that he found himself beyond pale reality with visions, voices, and high strangeness penetrating the virgin veil of polite familiarity. Keel might not make it onto an academic list of anthropologists, but like any true correspondent, his means and methodology were no different than those of a good ethnographer. He stepped into the reality of his investigations as a journalist willing to experience his subject matter head on. Rather than assuming he already knew, he sought out experience. In earlier work, such as the fieldwork he did in North Africa for his first book, Jaboo , he experienced foreign cultures with the intensity of an integrated outsider, and when Forteana came calling, he was no less willing to enter the role of participant observer. Quite vocal on how often these experiences are attended by fraud or foul play, he was still, to his dying day, a believer. Keel knew that the narrative structure of anomalous activity is often more important than the scientific veracity of its evidence. In exploring stories of unexplained events, the interplay of coincidence and phenomena can develop far beyond what any rational theory can hold on to. For the small price of a verifiable reason, Keel drank deep from the well of weirdness and found that common daylight does not illuminate every fold in space and time. INEFFICIENT TRUTHS Those enmeshed in corporations and economic entities like to have their schedules set and secure as they sew the seams of our daily lives. Our society’s legal and judicial branches enjoy clarity so linguistically profuse it’s nearly incomprehensible. Commoditized science prefers things logically sound, with results reducible to future funding. The narrative nature of unexplained phenomena is not easily dealt with when every functional facet of our society finds itself seeking efficiency at all cost. Stories ramble and wind their way toward uncertain conclusions, the part that can be told ends where words fail to follow, where the movement that sparked the speech slides beyond time. Anomalous phenomena break the façade of linear inquiry, eschewing sound ratios for absurd angles. Mundane science falters when experience is not explained by facts. It’s difficult to secure funding to study slippery synchronicities, especially when the results that do surface are not easily monetized. There is no measurable rationale for some stories, and yet they deeply affect how we live our lives, and how we understand and correlate events that we find ourselves party to on our journey. Sometimes stories get a bit odd: John Keel arrives on a West Virginia porch during a rainy night to ask directions and gets mistaken for the devil himself, come like a black dog to announce some future doom; and a few months later a bridge in town collapses killing over three dozen people. TRANSPERSONAL ANTHROPOLOGY Paranthropology 1 has emerged as a way to look at these phenomena through their cultural and personal evidence, a fluid yet rational approach that focuses on experiential and sensual relationships rather than seeking to secure a complex causal theory for the materialization of mysteries. It is a specialized version of what the anthropologist Charles Laughlin calls “transpersonal anthropology,” 2 explicitly looking at events and experiences that fall under the purview of what is commonly called paranormal. It is anthropology as seen through Hecate’s mirror. Jack Hunter, who runs the Journal of Anthropological Approaches to the Paranormal , did a study of Spiritualism for his thesis. Rather than just look at the movement’s history or conduct a sociological survey, he actually went in and trained with Spiritualist groups in order to enter a mediumistic trance state. 3 This kind of integrative approach is not common in a field harried by virulent skeptics quick to call undo prejudice on any investigator willing to suspend his or her assumptions long enough to enter the liminal world. Using the methods of ethnography to explore anomalous phenomena is not part of the regular procedures of parapsychology. Since the late 1960s and early 1970s, parapsychology has sought objective verification for phenomena in the laboratory rather than cultivating the experience as a participant in the field. The change in methodology that paranthropology presents mirrors approaches that have been successful for researchers exploring indigenous societies and social groups, especially in the areas of ritual and magic. Anthropology has long been the home of fringe experience. Researchers encountering other cultures are met with belief systems and experiences that run counter to their own expectations. Ethnography and immersion have proven the most successful means for overcoming this, by allowing researchers to fully explore a culture from within its own understanding. Bias is a given and provides no ground to dismiss the reality of the complex interplay of elements when the storytellers themselves enter the story. The power of this method can be seen in the resurgence of interest in the use of psychedelics for therapeutic means. What Western science failed to embrace in the laboratory fifty years ago has been forced into recognition by anthropologists and researchers who have consistently shown that psychedelics play a major role in the cohesion of many indigenous societies and, to the embarrassment of fundamentalists of any persuasion, by successful leaders who have confessed that some of their greatest insights have come while under the influence of a psychedelic experience or substance.
